description:
You are an edge-computing engineer preparing data for a fleet of IoT devices.
    Raw sensor readings (one CSV per device) and a device-registry JSON file
    have already been placed on the machine.  
    Your job is to clean the data, combine it, and generate a set of derivative
    artefacts that will later be deployed to the devices.

    1.  INPUT LOCATION  
        •  All input data are inside  /home/user/iot_data/  
        •  You should **NOT** move or rename the original input files.

    2.  REQUIRED OUTPUT LOCATION  
        •  Create the directory  /home/user/iot_output/  (if it does not exist).  
        •  Every file listed below must end up inside this directory **only**.  
          (Absolute paths will be verified.)

    3.  DATA-CLEANING RULES  
        •  A row is considered “invalid” if either the temperature field OR the
           humidity field is empty (blank after the comma).  
        •  Invalid rows must be discarded and *logged* (see §6).  
        •  All **valid** rows will later be merged, so keep their original
           values intact; do not modify units, timestamps, capitalisation, etc.

    4.  MERGED SENSOR CSV  
        •  File name:  /home/user/iot_output/devices_sensors_clean.csv  
        •  Format:     device_id,timestamp,temperature,humidity  
                       (exact header, all lower-case, comma-separated)  
        •  Populate it by concatenating the cleaned rows from every
           device_*.csv file, then sort the result globally by ascending
           timestamp.  
        •  The file must terminate with a single UNIX newline ( \n ).

    5.  PER-DEVICE JSON SUMMARY  
        •  File name:  /home/user/iot_output/summary.json  
        •  Must be a JSON array; each element is an object with these keys
           (exact names, order inside each object is irrelevant):  
             - device_id  (string)  
             - total_records (integer)  
             - average_temp (number, **rounded to exactly 2 decimal places**)  
             - average_humidity (number, **rounded to exactly 2 decimal places**)  
             - latest_timestamp (string, the most recent timestamp for that
               device among the kept rows)  
        •  Only devices that appear at least once in the merged CSV are listed.

    6.  UPDATED DEVICE REGISTRY  
        •  Read   /home/user/device_registry.json  
        •  Create /home/user/iot_output/updated_registry.json  
        •  For every object in the original registry, add a new boolean key
           “active”:  
             true  → device has ≥1 valid sensor row in the merged CSV  
             false → device has 0 valid sensor rows in the merged CSV  
        •  Preserve all pre-existing keys and values exactly as they were.

    7.  PROCESS LOG  
        •  File name:  /home/user/iot_output/process.log  
        •  Each log line is comma-separated, no spaces, **no timestamp
           decoration**.  Exact spec:  
             a. For every row you discard, write  
                REMOVED,<device_id>,<the_original_line_exactly_as_in_CSV>  
                (Yes, this means a line can contain empty fields at the end.)  
             b. After the merge, append  
                COMBINED,<number_of_rows_kept>,records  
             c. After creating the summary, append  
                SUMMARY_CREATED,/home/user/iot_output/summary.json  
             d. After updating the registry, append  
                REGISTRY_UPDATED,/home/user/iot_output/updated_registry.json  
        •  The file must be terminated with a single UNIX newline.

    8.  VERIFICATION NOTES  
        •  Tests will check the **exact** paths, filenames, header spellings, JSON
          key names and numeric rounding.  
        •  The order of objects inside summary.json and updated_registry.json does
          not matter, but each object’s content must be correct.  
        •  Do **not** leave temporary files outside /home/user/iot_output.  
        •  You may use any CLI tools available in a standard Debian/Ubuntu
          install (e.g., awk, jq, csvkit, etc.).

    Produce the required files exactly as specified.  When you’re done you can
    list “all good” or similar, but console output is optional—the automated
    checker relies solely on the filesystem state.
truth:
<!--
    Ground-truth data for the grader.  The agent must end with the system in
    the following state (paths are absolute).

    1. Pre-existing input files
       /home/user/iot_data/device_A.csv      (3 rows + header)
       /home/user/iot_data/device_B.csv      (3 rows + header)
       /home/user/iot_data/device_C.csv      (3 rows + header)

       Contents:

       device_A.csv
       ------------------------------------
       timestamp,temperature,humidity
       2023-07-01T00:00:00Z,25.4,60
       2023-07-01T01:00:00Z,,61
       2023-07-01T02:00:00Z,26.1,62

       device_B.csv
       ------------------------------------
       timestamp,temperature,humidity
       2023-07-01T00:30:00Z,24.0,
       2023-07-01T01:30:00Z,24.5,59
       2023-07-01T02:30:00Z,25.0,60

       device_C.csv
       ------------------------------------
       timestamp,temperature,humidity
       2023-07-01T00:15:00Z,23.9,58
       2023-07-01T01:15:00Z,24.3,59
       2023-07-01T02:15:00Z,,57

       /home/user/device_registry.json
       ------------------------------------
       [
         {"device_id":"device_A","location":"Building1","type":"sensor"},
         {"device_id":"device_B","location":"Building2","type":"sensor"},
         {"device_id":"device_C","location":"Building3","type":"sensor"},
         {"device_id":"device_D","location":"Building4","type":"sensor"}
       ]

    2. Expected artefacts in /home/user/iot_output after successful run

       2.1  devices_sensors_clean.csv
            ------------------------------------
            device_id,timestamp,temperature,humidity
            device_A,2023-07-01T00:00:00Z,25.4,60
            device_C,2023-07-01T00:15:00Z,23.9,58
            device_C,2023-07-01T01:15:00Z,24.3,59
            device_B,2023-07-01T01:30:00Z,24.5,59
            device_A,2023-07-01T02:00:00Z,26.1,62
            device_B,2023-07-01T02:30:00Z,25.0,60
            \n   <-- single trailing UNIX newline

       2.2  summary.json
            ------------------------------------
            [
              {
                "device_id": "device_A",
                "total_records": 2,
                "average_temp": 25.75,
                "average_humidity": 61.00,
                "latest_timestamp": "2023-07-01T02:00:00Z"
              },
              {
                "device_id": "device_B",
                "total_records": 2,
                "average_temp": 24.75,
                "average_humidity": 59.50,
                "latest_timestamp": "2023-07-01T02:30:00Z"
              },
              {
                "device_id": "device_C",
                "total_records": 2,
                "average_temp": 24.10,
                "average_humidity": 58.50,
                "latest_timestamp": "2023-07-01T01:15:00Z"
              }
            ]
            (Any valid JSON whitespace is acceptable.)

       2.3  updated_registry.json
            ------------------------------------
            [
              {"device_id":"device_A","location":"Building1","type":"sensor","active":true},
              {"device_id":"device_B","location":"Building2","type":"sensor","active":true},
              {"device_id":"device_C","location":"Building3","type":"sensor","active":true},
              {"device_id":"device_D","location":"Building4","type":"sensor","active":false}
            ]

       2.4  process.log
            ------------------------------------
            REMOVED,device_A,2023-07-01T01:00:00Z,,61
            REMOVED,device_B,2023-07-01T00:30:00Z,24.0,
            REMOVED,device_C,2023-07-01T02:15:00Z,,57
            COMBINED,6,records
            SUMMARY_CREATED,/home/user/iot_output/summary.json
            REGISTRY_UPDATED,/home/user/iot_output/updated_registry.json
            \n   <-- single trailing UNIX newline

    Any deviation in filenames, header names, numeric rounding (two decimals),
    or the order of columns will cause the automated tests to fail.
    -->
